content.full_text:
AC Transit
Alameda-Contra Costa Transit District

1600 Franklin Street, Oakland, California 94612 ☐ (510) 891-4827

Dec 28, 1992
December 28, 1992

Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

RE: Request For A Formal Advice Letter For
Director Pat Piras

Dear Commissioners:

I have been authorized by AC Transit Director Pat Piras
to request a formal written advice letter from the Commission
pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and Regulation 18329
regarding several potential conflict of interest situations in
which she may be involved. The mailing address of Director Piras
is listed above.

Background Information About AC Transit: AC Transit is
a special district created pursuant to PUC Section 24561, et seq.
for the purpose of providing mass public transportation to both
Alameda and Contra Costa counties. The Board of Directors is
composed of seven members, five are elected from wards, and two
are elected at large. Director Piras, who was elected to the
Board in November, 1990 and represents Ward IV which consists of
Castro Valley, the City of San Leandro (except that portion lying
north of the San Leandro Creek), San Lorenzo and those portions
of the City of Hayward lying generally east of a line following
the Southern Pacific tracks southward from the San Leandro city
line to west Jackson Street, to I-880, to the Union City limit.
The Board is the legislative body of the District which
determines all questions regarding District policy.

Background Information About Director Piras: Director
Piras has a business which provides transportation consulting
services to other public agencies, such as counties and cities in
the Bay Area and elsewhere in California. Her expertise includes
compliance with the Americans With Disabilities Act (ADA) and
paratransit issues. She is a sole proprietor but often utilizes
other individuals and firms as subcontractors in providing service
to public agencies.

In addition, she has recurring relationships acting as
a subcontractor to other private consulting associations and
businesses that provide transportation consulting services to
various public sector clients, primarily in California. As a
result, Director Piras is often faced with potential conflict of
interest situations when the issues she has an opportunity to
work on as a consultant or subcontractor dovetail with issues
addressed by the Board of Directors at AC Transit.

Letter to FPPC Dec. 28, 1992
on behalf of Director Piras
Page 2


        The following are a number factual situations which may
present a potential conflict of interest to Director Piras under
the  Political  Reform  Act.    Each  situation  is  described
separately, followed by the questions you are requested to
address.

SITUATION NO. 1 - ADA TASK FORCE SUB-CONSULTANT WORK

FACTS:

        Director Piras has been asked to participate on the
Regional ADA Eligibility Task Force (the Task Force) created by
the Metropolitan Transportation Commission (MTC).*1 The members
of the Task Force include operators, users and consultants.
Participation is strictly voluntary.  The purpose of the task
force is to develop recommendations for criteria to determine who
will be eligible for paratransit service.*2 Once accepted by
MTC, the recommendations will be submitted to each fixed route
operator for adoption, including AC Transit.  The ultimate goal
is to have consistent criteria throughout the nine-county Bay
Area.

        Because of Director Piras' expertise in this area, she
has been approached by a private consulting firm and offered the
opportunity to be paid as a subcontractor for the time she spends
working on the Task Force issues.  The consulting firm would pay
her fees from funds received from public agencies on the primary
contract.

_________________
*1.    MTC is the regional transportation agency which
oversees and coordinates public transportation for the Bay
Area.  It oversees and approves the short and long range
transportation plans of AC Transit.  Additionally, it is
responsible for approving certain grants and allocating state
funds between competing Bay Area transit agencies, including AC
Transit.
*2.    Paratransit service is a special transportation
service which is provided to individuals who are disabled and
unable to utilize mass public transportation services.
Paratransit is an expensive service to provide.   Thus,
determining who would qualify for such service is critical for
all the agencies involved.

Letter to FPPC Dec. 28, 1992
on behalf of Director Piras
Page 3


Questions for the Commission:

     1.  If Director Piras were a paid subcontractor for the
work she does on the Task Force, would this create a conflict of
interest under the Political Reform Act? More specifically:

     a.  Would the private primary consulting firm be
         considered Director Piras' "source of income"
         under the Political Reform Act?

     b.  Does the answer to "a" change if the primary
         contractor is able to prove that it pays fees
         to Director Piras from funds received from a
         public agency?

     c.  Would Director Piras have a conflict of
         interest based on "nexus" (Regulation
         18702.1(d)) if she participates in the District
         Board's decision regarding the adoption of
         MTC's detransit eligibility criteria if she
         is paid by the consulting firm for her
         participation on the Task Force which
         recommended the criteria for MTC?

     2.  Would Director Piras have any conflict of interest
if she voluntarily worked on the Task Force?

SITUATION NO. 2 - SANTA CLARA COUNTY TRANSPORTATION AGENCY
PRIVATE SECTOR ANALYSIS:

FACTS:

     Director Piras has the opportunity to bid on a proposal
offered by the Santa Clara County Transportation Agency
(hereafter SCCTA). The project includes reviewing and analyzing
"comparable" private sector contracts that other transit agencies
(particularly those in the Bay Area) have entered into. The
analysis would include the use of cost-allocation methodology to
compare the cost and service provided in private contracts with
other agencies to the service provided internally by SCCTA.
Recommendations regarding the type of service which is best
provided by private companies, how to effectively control the
service provided, as well as the terms and conditions that should
be included in contracts with private companies are part of the
analysis.

Letter to FPPC Dec. 28, 1992
on behalf of Director Piras
Page 4


     Currently AC Transit is the lead agency along with
SCCTA and other agencies in a consortium which privately
contracts for transit service across the Dumbarton Bridge. This
would be one of the contracts that the analysis would also
consider. If Director Piras' bid is selected by SCCTA, it is
possible that she might make recommendations to SCCTA regarding
the Dumbarton contract, a contract which would probably come
before the AC Transit Board at the time of its renewal.

QUESTIONS:

     1. Would the consulting fees paid directly to Director
Piras by SCCTA be considered "income" under the Political Reform
Act since SCCTA is a public agency?

     2. If so, would she be prohibited from participating
in any District Board decision regarding the Dumbarton Bridge
private contract if she makes specific recommendations to SCCTA
regarding provisions in that contract?

     3. Would the answer to question number two change if
Director Piras' recommendations to SCCTA regarding privatization
contracts were general in nature rather than specifically
addressing currently existing contracts, such as the Dumbarton
Bridge contract?

SITUATION NO. 3 - CCCTA STUDY ON SERVICE RELIABILITY

FACTS:

     Director Piras would like to bid on a proposal being
offered by Central Contra Costa Transit Authority (hereafter
"CCCTA") to conduct a study assessing how the agency collects and
reports indicators on service reliability. This would include
assessing current procedures used to measure schedule adherence
for both fixed-route and paratransit service, reviewing the
methodology used to determine and record missed trips, reviewing
scheduled running times for all routes and evaluating the current
BART-bus coordination. In addition, the proposers would be
expected to develop recommendations to improve CCCTA performance
based on the evaluation.

Letter to FPPC Dec. 28, 1992
on behalf of Director Piras
Page 5


Both AC Transit and CCCTA compete for funds from
Measure C *3, the Federal Transporation Agency and Bridge
tolls. In addition, the AC Transit Board periodically deals with
issues with respect to BART.

QUESTIONS:

1. If Director Piras' bid is accepted by CCCTA, would
her consulting fees be considered income under the Political
Reform Act?

2. If Director Piras is a subcontractor for a primary
contractor whose bid is accepted by the CCCTA, would her
subcontracting fees be considered income under the Political
Reform Act?

3. If the answers to questions one or two are yes,
would Director Piras be disqualified from participating in any
District Board decisions regarding:

a. The funds which both agencies compete for
if the work for CCCTA involves
recommendations involving those funds?

b. AC Transit routes which connect to BART
stations which may be addressed in the
work required for CCCTA?

SITUATION NO. 4 - ADA POLICY COMMITTEE:

FACTS:

Director Piras is an alternate, but active, member of
an Americans With Disabilities Act (ADA) Policy Committee
(hereafter "Committee") which is comprised of two directors
from each of four regional transit agencies, specially, AC
Transit, BART, Union City Transit, and Livermore-Amador Valley
Transit Agency (LAVTA). The purpose of the committee is to
devise a plan to coordinate ADA service in Alameda County. The
plan is to include general provisions applicable to all the
agencies as well as provisions specific to each agency. WTC
assisted the agencies in negotiating the Memorandum of
Understanding which established the committee. The members are
not compensated for serving on the committee.

*3. Measure C is a local one-half (1/2) cent sales tax
measure for transportation projects approved by Contra Costa
County voters in 1988.

Letter to FPPC Dec. 28, 1992
on behalf of Director Piras
Page 6


     One of the operators represented on the committee,
LAVTA, acts as a lead and has received funds from MTC to hire a
consultant to design the regional ADA plan for the committee to
consider.  The successful bidder will devise a policy for both
the region as well as the individual operators.  The bids were
solicited by LAVTA which will ultimately determine which
consultant to hire.  However, a committee made up of staff
members from each of the respective regional agencies involved
will interview the consultants who bid on the project and will
make a recommendation to LAVTA on which bidder to hire.

     Presently only three bids for the project have been
received.  Because of the "Minority owned business enterprise"
(MBE) and "women owned business enterprise" (WBE) requirements,
all three of the primary bidding firms have submitted proposals
with subcontracting firms and individuals.  Director Piras has
connections with two of the proposed bidders as follows:

     Bidder No. 1:  The prime bidder is
          currently a subcontractor for Director
          Piras for a consulting project she is
          doing for a public agency in Los Angeles
          County;

     Bidder No. 2:  Director Piras was a
          subcontractor for the primary bidder in
          1989 before she became a Director at AC
          Transit.  She also had a business
          association with their subcontractor back
          in 1989, but no longer considers them
          competent to do this type of work.

     If the staff committee recommends to LAVTA that Bidder
No. 2 be selected, Director Piras wants to suggest to the AC
Transit Board that it officially withdraw its representation on
the committee and petition MTC to receive a certain percentage of
the funds allotted to LAVTA to hire its own consultant to work
out its own ADA plan in connection with the region.

QUESTIONS:

     1.  If the regional staff committee decides to
recommend that LAVTA accept Bidder No. 2, would Director Piras
have a conflict of interest if she participates in an AC Transit
Board discussion regarding the recommendation?

Letter to FPPC Dec. 28, 1992
on behalf of Director Piras
Page 7


2.  Would the answer to the above question be different
if her business relationship with the primary and subcontractor
had occurred within twelve months of the decision?

3.  Would the answer to question No. 1 change if the
discussion and action concerned Bidder No. 1?

SITUATION NO. 5 - UNIVERSITY OF MASSACHUSETTS FERRY WORKSHOP:

FACTS:

     Director Piras has been asked to be a facilitator for a
workshop on water transit issues relating to the ADA being put on
by the University of Massachusetts.  She would be paid an
"honorarium" for contributing approximately 2/3 of the conference
agenda.  She would also be expected to attend most of the other
sessions.  Although the University is the responsible entity for
the workshop, a private consulting firm is involved in making
arrangements for the conference, including soliciting speakers.
One of the part-time principals of this private consulting firm
is an AC Transit employee.  Director Piras has had no contact
with this employee and does not know if the employee suggested
that she be contacted to be the facilitator of the conference.

QUESTIONS:

     1.  Would Director Piras have a conflict of interest if
she participates in the workshop assuming that his participation
is the result of the AC Transit employee's suggestion?

     2.  Would the "honorarium" that Director Piras would
receive from the University of Massachusetts fall within the
definition of Title 2, California Code of Regulations, Section
18728, if she is not being paid for speaking as a public
official, but instead is being paid for professional and
technical services rendered?

     If any additional information is required, please feel
free to call my office at (510) 891-4827.  Thank you for your
consideration of these issues.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Kenneth C. Scheideig
                                        General Counsel

cc:  Director Piras

KW:dyw

AC Transit
Alameda-Contra Costa Transit District

1600 Franklin Street, Oakland, California 94612 □ (510) 891-4827

CONFIDENTIAL

(Subject To Attorney-Client Privilege - Not For Distribution)

March 25, 1992

TO:        KENNETH C. SCHEIDIG, GENERAL COUNSEL

FROM:      KAT WELLMAN, ATTORNEY I

SUBJECT:   CONFLICT OF INTEREST - ADA POLICY COMMITTEE

FACTS:

Pat Piras is an alternate member*1 of an ADA Policy
Committee (hereinafter "committee") which is comprised of two
directors from each of the regional transit agencies - AC
Transit, BART, Union City Transit, and Livermore-Amador Valley
Transit Agency (LAVTA). The purpose of the ADA Policy Committee
is to devise a regional ADA plan to coordinate ADA service in the
region. The plan will potentially include not only general
provisions applicable to all the agencies, but also provisions
specific to each agency. The agencies in
negotiating the Memorandum of Understanding which established the
committee. The members are not compensated for serving on the
committee.

One of the operators, LAVTA, has received funds from
WTC in order to hire a consultant to design the regional ADA plan
for the committee to consider. The bids were solicited by LAVTA
which will ultimately determine which consultant to hire.
However, a committee made up of staff members from each of the
respective regional agencies involved will interview the
consultants who have bid on the project and will make a
recommendation to LAVTA on which bidder to hire. The interviews
are scheduled to take place today and Pat believes she will know
the recommended decision before today's Board meeting.

*1.  Initially, Michael Winter and Pat Piras were the
designated directors representing AC Transit on the committee.
However, now the official representatives are Michael Winter and
Joseph Bischofberger and Pat is the designated alternate.
However, since Michael Winter never or rarely does to the
meetings, Pat is able to be very active and vocal on the
committee. If Michael Winter does attend, she becomes merely an
observer.

GC:PT  IG, 9I 3LHJ         zod zpD

Memo to Ken Scheidig
RE: Conflict of Interest - ADA Policy Committee
Page 2

According to Pat there were only three bids for the
project. Because of the DBE and WBE requirements, all three of
the primary bidding firms have submitted a proposal to use
particular subcontractors. Pat's connection to the three
proposed bids is as follows:

Bid #1: Pat has never heard of either the primary
bidder or the subcontractor (which amazed her);

Bid #2: The prime bidder is currently a subcontractor
for Pat for a consulting project she is doing for a Los Angeles
entity;

Bid #3: Pat was a subcontractor for the prime bidder,
Crane, in 1989 before she became a Director for AC Transit. She
also was connected to the subcontractor Nelson & ? back in 1989
but had a major falling out with them and no longer considers
them competent to do this type of work.

Because the successful bidder will be devising a policy
for both the region as well as the individual operators, Pat is
very adamant that she does not want the subcontractor which she
considers incompetent having any involvement with proposals which
may be before AC Transit for adoption. As a result, she has
included an agenda item for today's Board meeting which will
allow her to discuss the matter with the Board if the regional
agencies' staff committee chooses to recommend the Crane bid to
LAVTA. Today's meeting is the only opportunity Pat will have to
raise the issue before the AC Transit Board before the LAVTA
Board of Directors makes a final decision whether or not to
accept the staff committee's recommendation.

If the Crane bid is not recommended by the staff
committee, Pat will have no need to discuss anything with the
Board. However, if the Crane bid is recommended by the staff
committee, Pat wants to suggest to the Board that it withdraw
from the MOU, thus removing AC Transit from the ADA policy
committee. AC Transit could then petition WTC to receive a
certain percentage of the funds allotted to LAVTA to hire its own
consultant to work out its own ADA plan in connection with the
region.*2

*2. This opinion is based on the accuracy of the
information which has been provided. If any of the information
should change, then the conclusions in this opinion would have to
be reconsidered.

OC:PT 26, 9I WLIH CDM 2P0

Memo to Ken Scheiding
RE: Conflict of Interest - ADA Policy Committee
Page 3


ISSUE:

If the regional agencies' staff committee decides to recommend
that LAVTA accept the Crane bid, would any conflict of interest
statutes or common law doctrines prohibit Pat Piras from
participating in any discussions about the recommended bid in
today's AC Transit Board of Directors meeting?

OPINION: NO. Under the Fair Political Practices Act (the Act) a
conflict of interest would only occur if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the Board action could materially affect a
financial interest of Pat. In this case, the only financial
interest that would appear to exist is a "source of income" to Pat.
Since Pat has not worked for Crane in
the last twelve months, the firm would not be considered a source
of income under Section 87103 of the Act. Although Pat is
currently working with "Bidder #2, she is using them as her
subcontractor in that project. Thus, she is the source of income
to them, and not vice versa. This type of relationship is not
covered by the Act.

DISCUSSION:

1. CONFLICT OF INTEREST UNDER THE POLITICAL REFORM ACT OF 1974 -

The Political Reform Act of 1974 (the Act) governs non-
contractual conflicts of interest.*3 The conflict of interest
provisions are found at Government Code Section 87100, et seq.

Section 87100 sets forth the general prohibition against misuse
of public office where a conflict of interest exists.

"No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a government decision in which he knows
or has reason to know he has a financial interest."


*3. Government Code Section 1090 governs contractual
conflicts of interest. However, since no contract will actually
be before the AC Transit Board of Directors, this section is not
applicable and will therefore not be discussed.


IC:P1 £6.9I uMH   PDu ZPO

Memo to Ken Scheidig
RE: Conflict of Interest - ADA Policy Committee
Page 4

A financial interest is identified in Section 87103 which
provides, in part:

"An official has a financial interest in a decision within
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on the official or a member of
his or her immediate family or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public has a direct or
    indirect investment worth one thousand ($1000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than
    loans by a commercial lending institution in the
    regular course of business on terms available to the
    public without regard to official status, aggregating
    two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
    provided to, received by or promised to the public
    official within 12 months prior to the time when the
    decision is made.

In order for a conflict of interest to arise, four
elements derived from the above sections must be present: (1)
participating in or influencing a governmental decision; (2)
reasonable foreseeability that the decision will affect the
official's financial interest; (3) the existence of a material
financial interest; and (4) the financial effect must be
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. If any
one of these elements is missing, there is no conflict of
interest under the Act.

APPLICATION OF THE ACT. In applying this criteria to
Pat's situation it does not appear as though she has a "financial
interest" as defined by the Act. A financial interest would only
occur if either of the two bidders Pat has or is currently
working with meet the statutory definition of a "source of
income." This, however, is not the case. According to the facts
presented by Pat, she has not worked for Crane (Bid #3) since
1989 which is beyond the twelve month limit required by

[illegible] [illegible] [illegible]
IE:P1 ZS.91 UdW SPdZ Z8d

Memo to Ken Scheidig
RE: Conflict of Interest - ADA Policy Committee
Page 5


section 87103(c). Although Pat is currently working with the Bid
#2 consultant, she has hired that firm or individual to work for
her in a subcontractor. Thus, she would be the source of income
for them, and not vice versa. Since the Act does not cover such
situations, no conflict of interest would exist.

The only other financial interest that might be
potentially affected would be Pat's consulting business.
However, it would appear that unless Pat was involved either
directly or indirectly in the consulting work for LAVTA, her
consulting business would not be affected by any decision either
the District or even LAVTA could make regarding the bid.

Unless a financial interest is affected, there is no
conflict under the Act. Pat would thus be free to raise or
discuss the issue if presented in tonight's board meeting.

(piras1)

MAR-18-93 THU 8:42                                                P.02

AC Transit
Alameda-Contra Costa Transit District

1600 Franklin Street, Oakland, California 94612 □ (415) 891-4777

February 28, 1992

TO:      DIRECTOR PIRAS

FROM:    KENNETH C. SCHEIDIG, GENERAL COUNSEL

RE:      CONFLICT OF INTEREST OPINION - ADA TASK FORCE AND
         CONSULTING WORK

FACTS:

You have requested a legal opinion analyzing whether your
consulting services for various clients and projects create a
conflict of interest with your position as a Director of AC
Transit.

You have provided written materials regarding your consulting
activities together with verbal information which has been used by
this office in evaluating your request.¹

Your consulting services include work involving various
transportation and ADA paratransit issues. Your clients are often
other public agencies, such as counties and cities in the Bay Area
and elsewhere in California. In addition, you have recurring
contractual relationships with other consulting associations and
businesses that provide services to various public section clients,
primarily in California.

In addition, you were recently appointed to the Regional ADA
Eligibility Task Force (the Task Force). The Task Force was
established by MTC to develop recommendations for criteria to
determine who will be eligible for paratransit service. Once
accepted by MTC, the recommendations will be submitted to each
fixed route operator for adoption. The members of the Task Force
include operators, users and consultants. Participation is
strictly voluntary. However, because of your expertise in this
area you could be paid as a subcontractor by a consulting firm for
the time you spend on the Task Force. The consulting firm would
pay your fees from funds received from public agencies on the
primary contract.

You have inquired whether your participation on the Task Force
creates a conflict of interest with your position as a Director on
the AC Transit Board.

_______________
¹ This opinion is based on the accuracy of the information
which has been provided. If any of the information should change
then the conclusions of this opinion would have to be reconsidered.

MAR-18-93 THU 8:43                                                P.03


                    Director Piras
                    Re: Conflict of Interest Opinion
                    February 28, 1992
                    Page 2


     ISSUES:

          1.     Would any applicable conflict of interest statutes or
     common law doctrines be violated by your paid consulting services
     or your volunteer work for and membership in various organizations
     while simultaneously holding the office of AC Transit Director?

          2.     Would any applicable conflict of interest statutes or
     common law doctrines be violated by your continuing to serve as a
     member of the Regional ADA Eligibility Task Force while
     simultaneously holding the office of AC Transit Director if:

          a.     You charge for the services you render to the Task
     Force?

          b.     You volunteer your services to the Task Force?


     ANSWER:

     1.a.     A definite answer regarding your paid consulting
     services cannot be provided without a review of the specific facts
     as various situations arise.    Each situation would have to be
     analyzed under the noncontractual conflicts provisions of the
     Political Reform Act of 1974 (the Act), the contractual conflicts
     provisions contained in section 1090 et seq., as well as the common
     law doctrine of conflicts of interests.

     1.b.     Whether your voluntary work and/or participation in
     various organizations would give rise to a common law
     incompatibility of office issue would depend on whether the
     position you occupy in each organization is a "public office".

     2.a.     If you receive income from a consulting firm for work on
     recommendations submitted to the Task Force, then any participation
     by you in deliberations by the AC Transit Board of Directors
     relating to those recommendations could be considered a conflict of
     interest under the Act.

     2.b.     No.  If you volunteer your services to the Task Force,
     none of the conflict of interest statutes would apply since they
     are all based on a financial interest.  Your membership on the Task
     Force is not a public office and therefore does not present a
     conflict of interest in violation of the common law doctrine of
     incompatibility of offices based on the information which has been
     provided to this office.

MAR-18-93 THU 8:44                                                P.04


                    Director Piras
                    Re: Conflict of Interest Opinion
                    February 28, 1992
                    Page 3


DISCUSSION:

In order to answer the various issues which have been raised this
opinion will be divided into two parts.  The first part will
addify various laws or legal principles which would govern
your activities.  The second part will apply those principles to
the issues which have been raised.

I.     APPLICABLE LAWS OR LEGAL PRINCIPLES.

       A.     CONFLICT OF INTEREST

            1. The Political Reform Act of 1974 - Government Code
Section 87100 et sequitu.

The Political Reform Act of 1974 (the Act) governs non-contractual
conflicts of interest.  The conflict of interest provisions are
found at Government Code sections 87100 et seq.¹

Section 87100 sets forth the general prohibition against misuse of
public office where a conflict of interest exists.

       "No public official at any level of state or local
       government shall make, participate in making or in any
       way attempt to use his official position to influence a
       governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to
       know he has a financial interest."

A financial interest can arise in a variety of situations.  They
are identified in section 87103.

       "An official has a financial interest in a decision
       within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
       foreseeable that the decision will have a material
       financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
       public generally, on the official or a member of his or
       her immediate family or on:

       (a)  Any business entity in which the public official has
       a direct or indirect investment worth one thousand
       dollars ($1,000) or more.


_________________________

¹ All references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated.

MAR-18-93 THU 8:44                                P.05

Director Piras
Re: Conflict of Interest Opinion
February 28, 1992
Page 4


     (b) Any real property in which the public official has
a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand dollars
($1,000) or more.

     (c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other
than loans by a commercial lending institution in the
regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided
to, received by or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

     (d) Any business entity in which the public official is
a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds
any position of management.

     (e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a
donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by,
or promised to the public official within 12 months prior
to the time when the decision is made.

For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest
means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or
dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf
a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which
the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent
children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-
percent interest or greater."

In order for a conflict of interest to arise four elements derived
from the above sections must be present: (1) participating in or
influencing a governmental decision; (2) reasonable foreseeability
that the decision will affect the official's financial interest;
(3) the existence of a material financial interest; and (4) the
financial effect must be distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally.

     (1) Participation in a governmental decision.    2 CCR,
section 18700 includes participating in discussions, responding to
comments, advising on making recommendations, voting on a matter,
obligating the agency or committing the agency to any course of
action, entering into a contractual agreement on behalf of the
agency and even determining whether not to act, unless that
determination is made because the official disqualifies him/herself
due to a conflict of interest.

MAR-18-93 THU 8:45                                P.06


        Director Piras
        Re: Conflict of Interest Opinion
        February 28, 1992
        Page 5


     (2) Reasonable foreseeability and financial interest.
An effect on an official's financial interest is foreseeable when
there is a substantial likelihood that it will ultimately occur as
a result of a governmental decision. That likelihood need not be
a "certainty", but it must be more than a "mere possibility".
Downey Carey v. Downey Community Development Commission (1987) 196
Cal.App.3d 983. The pertinent financial interests involved under
section 87103 are contained in subsections (a), (c) and (d) since
you are self-employed.

     (3) Material financial effect. Generally, the
materiality of the financial interest is dependent on the dollar
value involved.

        a. Direct financial interest. Title 2 of the
California Code of Regulations (CCR), section 18702.1 provides that
the effect of a decision is material if any person or entity which
has been a source of income to the official of $250 or more in the
preceding twelve months is directly involved in a decision before
the official's agency. Such a direct involvement occurs if the
affected person or entity initiates the proceeding, if it is a
named party in the proceeding or if it is the subject of the
proceeding before the governmental agency.    2 CCR, section
18702.1(b). If a primary consultant to whom you were a sub-
contractor was involved in any of the above situations, you would
have to abstain due to a conflict of interest. For example, the
District seeks an RFP for compliance with the Clean Air Act and the
contract is awarded to your primary consultant.     A conflict would
immediately arise.³

        b. Indirect financial interest. If a decision of
the AC Transit Board indirectly affected a project in which you had
an interest as a subcontractor you would not be able to participate
in or attempt to influence the decision if your business was
affected by the dollar limits identified in 2 CCR section
18702.2. For small business subsection (g) sets the impacts at an increase
or decrease in gross revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more;
or the decision would cause your business to incur or avoid
additional expenses or reduce or eliminate existing expenses by
$2,500 or more; or the decision will cause an increase or decrease
in value of assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more.


_______________
  ³ A violation of section 1090 also would exist.

MAR-18-93 THU 8:46                                    P.07

                    Director Piras
                    Re: Conflict of Interest Opinion
                    February 28, 1992
                    Page 6


          C.  Nexus.  The effect of a decision also is
deemed to be material if there is a nexus between the purpose for
which the official received $250 or more in income and the
governmental decision. 2 CCR section 18702.1(a)(1). Subsection
(d) defines "nexus" as follows:

     "There is a nexus between the purpose for which an
     official receives income and a governmental decision if
     the official receives income to achieve a goal or purpose
     which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by
     the decision."

     The determination whether a "nexus" exists, like all conflict of
interest questions, is fact based. For example, if the District
was considering paratransit guidelines which you helped to develop
as a paid subcontractor and their passage would assist in the
marketing of your business, it could be argued that the required
nexus exists. It is possible that a nexus might exist involving
work you have done as a volunteer if it can be shown that the
approval or denial of the work would affect your business. A
definitive answer is difficult to provide since there have been
very few rulings by the FPPC in this area.


     4.   Distinguishable from effect on the public.  A
conflict of interest can be avoided if the decision affects the
official's financial interest in substantially the same manner as
it will affect all members of the public or a significant segment
of the public. 2 CCR, section 18703.

     In summary, a conflict under the Act only exists if an official
makes or participates in a decision in which he/she has a financial
interest. Additionally, it must be reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on the official
which is distinguishable from its effect upon the public generally.

     B.   Common Law.

     Although the Political Reform Act would appear to have preempted
the common law doctrine against conflict of interest there is no
case law holding this position. Until the matter is decided a
court could apply both the statutory and common law doctrines in
determining a conflict of interest violation.

MAR-18-93 THU  8:46                                                P.08


y .

           Director Piras
           Re: Conflict of Interest Opinion
           February 28, 1992
           Page 7


     Under this doctrine "[p]ublic officers are obligated . . . [by
virtue of their office] to discharge their responsibilities with
integrity and fidelity." Terry v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d
198, 206. The doctrine can effect financial and nonfinancial
personal interests and would disqualify the official from "taking
any part in the discussion and vote regarding" the particular
matter. 26 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 5, 7 (1955).

     Since it is still an open question whether the courts would apply
the common law conflict of interest doctrine in the absence of a
statutory violation you should abstain from participating in any AC
Transit Board discussion or action concerning any recommendation or
action where you believe that you may not be able to meet the
common law conflict of interest test.

     If a conflict of interest should occur under either the Act or the
common law, the existence of the conflict would have to be
disclosed on the record and you would have to abstain from
participating in or attempting to influence the decision.

     C.  Section 1090 et sequitur.  These provisions govern
contractual conflicts of interests. They prohibit officials from
being financially interested in any contract made by them in their
official capacity or by any body or board of which they are
official members. Section 1090. Any contract in which a direct conflict
exists is void from its inception and cannot be saved. A remote
interest, defined in section 1091(b), is permissible if there is a
disclosure of the interest and non-participation.

     Board members are conclusively presumed to be involved in the
making of all contracts executed by the board or an agency under is
jurisdiction, even though they disqualify themselves from any and
all participation in the making of the contract. Thomson v. Call
(1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 645, 649.

     D.  Incompatibility of office.

     The common law prohibits the simultaneous holding of incompatible
public offices. In 66 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 176, 177-178 (1983) the

          4 If an incompatibility of offices exists the public officer
forfeits by operation of law the first office and retains the
second one. Thus, if an incompatibility existed and you were
appointed to your position after being elected to your seat on AC

MAR-18-93 THU 8:47                                    P.09


    Director Piras
    Re: Conflict of Interest Opinion
    February 28, 1992
    Page 8


common law rule was summarized, in part, as follows:

     "Offices are incompatible, in the absence of statutes
     adopting a contrary result, if there is any significant
     clash of duties or loyalties between the offices, if the
     dual office holding would be improper for reasons of
     public policy, or if either office exercises
     supervisory, auditory, or removal power over the other.
     (38 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 113 (1961).

In People ex el. Chapman v. Rapsey (1940) 14 Cal.2d 636 the court
held that "[t]wo offices are said to be incompatible when the
holder cannot in every instance discharge the duties of each."

However, in order for the common law rule to be applicable the
positions in question would have to constitute a "public office".
The California Attorney General in 68 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 337 (1985)
reviewed the leading case law regarding what constitutes a public
office for the purposes of the incompatibility doctrine and
concluded at page 342:

     ". . . a public office is a position in government (1)
     which is created or authorized by the Constitution or
     some law; (2) the tenure of which is continuing and
     permanent, not occasional or temporary; (3) in which the
     incumbent performs a public function for the public
     benefit and exercises some of the sovereign power of the
     state."

In addition, if a person merely advises policy makers the courts
and California Attorney General have ruled that the position is
probably not a public office. 56 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen.556, 561. See
also, Parker v. Riley (1941) 18 Cal.2d 83, 87; 42 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen.
93, 95 (1963).

II. APPLICATION OF LEGAL PRINCIPLES TO THE ISSUES.

There are three different types of decisions which could come
before the AC Transit Board of Directors as a result of your
consulting work:

     1.    Decisions which may potentially affect your consulting
           business;

_______________________
Transit's Board of Directors you would automatically forfeit your
seat on the AC Transit Board.

sections.question:
1. Would the consulting fees paid directly to Director Piras by SCCTA be considered "income" under the Political Reform Act since SCCTA is a public agency?

2. If so, would she be prohibited from participating in any District Board decision regarding the Dumbarton Bridge private contract if she makes specific recommendations to SCCTA regarding provisions in that contract?

3. Would the answer to question number two change if Director Piras’ recommendations to SCCTA regarding privatization contracts were general in nature rather than specifically addressing currently existing contracts, such as the Dumbarton Bridge contract?

SITUATION NO. 3 - CCCTA STUDY ON SERVICE RELIABILITY

FACTS:

Director Piras would like to bid on a proposal being offered by Central Contra Costa Transit Authority (hereafter "CCCTA") to conduct a study assessing how the agency collects and reports indicators on service reliability. This would include assessing current procedures used to measure schedule adherence for both fixed-route and paratransit service, reviewing the methodology used to determine and record missed trips, reviewing scheduled running times for all routes and evaluating the current BART-bus coordination. In addition, the proposers would be expected to develop recommendations to improve CCCTA performance based on the evaluation.

Both AC Transit and CCCTA compete for funds from Measure C *3, the Federal Transportation Agency and Bridge tolls. In addition, the AC Transit Board periodically deals with issues with respect to BART.

QUESTIONS:

1. If Director Piras’ bid is accepted by CCCTA, would her consulting fees be considered income under the Political Reform Act?

2. If Director Piras is a subcontractor for a primary contractor whose bid is accepted by the CCCTA, would her subcontracting fees be considered income under the Political Reform Act?

3. If the answers to questions one or two are yes, would Director Piras be disqualified from participating in any District Board decisions regarding:

a. The funds which both agencies compete for if the work for CCCTA involves recommendations involving those funds?

b. AC Transit routes which connect to BART stations which may be addressed in the work required for CCCTA?

SITUATION NO. 4 - ADA POLICY COMMITTEE:

FACTS:

Director Piras is an alternate, but active, member of an Americans With Disabilities Act (ADA) Policy Committee (hereinafter "committee") which is comprised of two directors from each of four regional transit agencies, specifically, AC Transit, BART, Union City Transit, and Livermore-Amador Valley Transit Agency (LAVTA). The purpose of the committee is to devise a plan to coordinate ADA service in Alameda County. The plan is to include general provisions applicable to all the agencies as well as provisions specific to each agency. MTC assisted the agencies in negotiating the Memorandum of Understanding which established the committee. The members are not compensated for serving on the committee.

*3. Measure C is a local one-half (1/2) cent sales tax measure for transportation projects approved by Contra Costa County voters in 1988.

One of the operators represented on the committee, LAVTA, acts as a lead and has received funds from MTC to hire a consultant to design the regional ADA plan for the committee to consider. The successful bidder will devise a policy for both the region as well as the individual operators. The bids were solicited by LAVTA which will ultimately determine which consultant to hire. However, a committee made up of staff members from each of the respective regional agencies involved will interview the consultants who bid on the project and will make a recommendation to LAVTA on which bidder to hire.

Presently only three bids for the project have been received. Because of the "minority owned business enterprise" (MBE) and "women owned business enterprise" (WBE) requirements, all three of the primary bidding firms have submitted proposals with subcontracting firms and individuals. Director Piras has connections with two of the proposed bidders as follows:

Bidder No. 1: The prime bidder is currently a subcontractor for Director Piras for a consulting project she is doing for a public agency in Los Angeles County;

Bidder No. 2: Director Piras was a subcontractor for the primary bidder in 1989 before she became a Director at AC Transit. She also had a business association with their subcontractor back in 1989, but no longer considers them competent to do this type of work.

If the staff committee recommends to LAVTA that Bidder No. 2 be selected, Director Piras wants to suggest to the AC Transit Board that it officially withdraw its representation on the committee and petition MTC to receive a certain percentage of the funds allotted to LAVTA to hire its own consultant to work out its own ADA plan in connection with the region.

QUESTIONS:

1. If the regional staff committee decides to recommend that LAVTA accept Bidder No. 2, would Director Piras have a conflict of interest if she participates in an AC Transit Board discussion regarding the recommendation?

2. Would the answer to the above question be different if her business relationship with the primary and subcontractor had occurred within twelve months of the decision?

3. Would the answer to question No. 1 change if the discussion and action concerned Bidder No. 1?

SITUATION NO. 5 - UNIVERSITY OF MASSACHUSETTS FERRY WORKSHOP:

FACTS:

Director Piras has been asked to be a facilitator for a workshop on water transit issues relating to the ADA being put on by the University of Massachusetts. She would be paid an "honorarium" for contributing approximately 23% of the conference agenda. She would also be expected to attend most of the other sessions. Although the University is the responsible entity for the workshop, a private consulting firm is involved in making arrangements for the conference, including soliciting speakers. One of the part-time principals of this private consulting firm is an AC Transit employee. Director Piras has had no contact with this employee and does not know if the employee suggested that she be contacted to be the facilitator of the conference.

QUESTIONS:

1. Would Director Piras have a conflict of interest if she participates in the workshop assuming that her participation is the result of the AC Transit employee's suggestion?

2. Would the "honorarium" that Director Piras would receive from the University of Massachusetts fall within the definition of Title 2, California Code of Regulations, Section 18728, if she is not being paid for speaking as a public official, but instead is being paid for professional and technical services rendered?

If any additional information is required, please feel free to call my office at (510) 891-4827. Thank you for your consideration of these issues.
sections.facts:
Director Piras has been asked to participate on the Regional ADA Eligibility Task Force (the Task Force) created by the Metropolitan Transportation Commission (MTC).*1 The members of the Task Force include operators, users and consultants. Participation is strictly voluntary. The purpose of the task force is to develop recommendations for criteria to determine who will be eligible for paratransit service.*2 Once accepted by MTC, the recommendations will be submitted to each fixed route operator for adoption, including AC Transit. The ultimate goal is to have consistent criteria throughout the nine-county Bay Area.

Because of Director Piras' expertise in this area, she has been approached by a private consulting firm and offered the opportunity to be paid as a subcontractor for the time she spends working on the Task Force issues. The consulting firm would pay her fees from funds received from public agencies on the primary contract.

*1. MTC is the regional transportation agency which oversees and coordinates public transportation for the Bay Area. It oversees and approves the short and long range transportation plans of AC Transit. Additionally, it is responsible for approving certain grants and allocating state funds between competing Bay Area transit agencies, including AC Transit.

*2. Paratransit service is a special transportation service which is provided to individuals who are disabled and unable to utilize mass public transportation services. Paratransit is an expensive service to provide. Thus, determining who would qualify for such service is critical for all the agencies involved.

Questions for the Commission:

1. If Director Piras were a paid subcontractor for the work she does on the Task Force, would this create a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act? More specifically:

a. Would the private primary consulting firm be considered Director Piras’ "source of income" under the Political Reform Act?

b. Does the answer to "a" change if the primary contractor is able to prove that it pays fees to Director Piras from funds received from a public agency?

c. Would Director Piras have a conflict of interest based on "nexus" (Regulation 18702.1(d)) if she participates in the District Board’s decision regarding the adoption of MTC’s paratransit eligibility criteria if she is paid by the consulting firm for her participation on the Task Force which recommended the criteria for MTC?

2. Would Director Piras have any conflict of interest if she voluntarily worked on the Task Force?

SITUATION NO. 2 - SANTA CLARA COUNTY TRANSPORTATION AGENCY PRIVATE SECTOR ANALYSIS:

FACTS:

Director Piras has the opportunity to bid on a proposal offered by the Santa Clara County Transportation Agency (hereafter SCCTA). The project includes reviewing and analyzing "comparable" private sector contracts that other transit agencies (particularly those in the Bay Area) have entered into. The analysis would include the use of cost-allocation methodology to compare the cost and service provided in private contracts with other agencies to the service provided internally by SCCTA. Recommendations regarding the type of service which is best provided by private companies, how to effectively control the service provided, as well as the terms and conditions that should be included in contracts with private companies are part of the analysis.

Currently AC Transit is the lead agency along with SCCTA and other agencies in a consortium which privately contracts for transit service across the Dumbarton Bridge. This would be one of the contracts that the analysis would also consider. If Director Piras’ bid is selected by SCCTA, it is possible that she might make recommendations to SCCTA regarding the Dumbarton contract, a contract which would probably come before the AC Transit Board at the time of its renewal.
sections.analysis:
1. CONFLICT OF INTEREST UNDER THE POLITICAL REFORM ACT OF 1974 -

The Political Reform Act of 1974 (the Act) governs non-contractual conflicts of interest.*3 The conflict of interest provisions are found at Government Code Section 87100, et seq.

Section 87100 sets forth the general prohibition against misuse of public office where a conflict of interest exists.

"No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a government decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest."

*3. Government Code Section 1090 governs contractual conflicts of interest. However, since no contract will actually be before the AC Transit Board of Directors, this section is not applicable and will therefore not be discussed.

A financial interest is identified in Section 87103 which provides, in part:

"An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public has a direct or indirect investment worth one thousand ($1000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

In order for a conflict of interest to arise, four elements derived from the above sections must be present: (1) participating in or influencing a governmental decision; (2) reasonable foreseeability that the decision will affect the official's financial interest; (3) the existence of a material financial interest; and (4) the financial effect must be distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. If any one of these elements is missing, there is no conflict of interest under the Act.

APPLICATION OF THE ACT. In applying this criteria to Pat's situation it does not appear as though she has a "financial interest" as defined by the Act. A financial interest would only occur if either of the two bidders Pat has or is currently working with meet the statutory definition of a "source of income." This, however, is not the case. According to the facts presented by Pat, she has not worked for Crane (Bid #3) since 1989 which is beyond the twelve month limit required by

section 87103(c). Although Pat is currently working with the Bid #2 consultant, she has hired that firm or individual to work for her as a subcontractor. Thus, she would be the source of income for them, and not vice versa. Since the Act does not cover such situations, no conflict of interest would exist.

The only other financial interest that might be potentially affected would be Pat's consulting business. However, it would appear that unless Pat was involved either directly or indirectly in the consulting work for LAVTA, her consulting business would not be affected by any decision either the District or even LAVTA could make regarding the bid.

Unless a financial interest is affected, there is no conflict under the Act. Pat would thus be free to raise or discuss the issue if presented in tonight's board meeting.
