(piras1)

February 28, 1992

TO: DIRECTOR PIRAS
FROM: KENNETH C. SCHEIDIG, GENERAL COUNSEL
RE: CONFLICT OF INTEREST OPINION - ADA TASK FORCE AND CONSULTING WORK

FACTS:

You have requested a legal opinion analyzing whether your consulting services for various clients and projects create a conflict of interest with your position as a Director of AC Transit.

You have provided written materials regarding your consulting activities together with verbal information which has been used by this office in evaluating your request.¹

Your consulting services include work involving various transportation and ADA paratransit issues. Your clients are often other public agencies, such as counties and cities in the Bay Area and elsewhere in California. In addition, you have recurring contractual relationships with other consulting associations and businesses that provide services to various public section clients, primarily in California.

In addition, you were recently appointed to the Regional ADA Eligibility Task Force (the Task Force). The Task Force was established by MTC to develop recommendations for criteria to determine who will be eligible for paratransit service. Once accepted by MTC, the recommendations will be submitted to each fixed route operator for adoption. The members of the Task Force include operators, users and consultants. Participation is strictly voluntary. However, because of your expertise in this area you could be paid as a subcontractor by a consulting firm for the time you spend on the Task Force. The consulting firm would pay your fees from funds received from public agencies on the primary contract.

You have inquired whether your participation on the Task Force creates a conflict of interest with your position as a Director on the AC Transit Board.

¹ This opinion is based on the accuracy of the information which has been provided. If any of the information should change then the conclusions of this opinion would have to be reconsidered.

Director Piras  
Re: Conflict of Interest Opinion  
February 28, 1992  

ISSUES:

1. Would any applicable conflict of interest statutes or common law doctrines be violated by your paid consulting services or your volunteer work for and membership in various organizations while simultaneously holding the office of AC Transit Director?

2. Would any applicable conflict of interest statutes or common law doctrines be violated by your continuing to serve as a member of the Regional ADA Eligibility Task Force while simultaneously holding the office of AC Transit Director if:

a. You charge for the services you render to the Task Force?

b. You volunteer your services to the Task Force?

ANSWER:

1.a. A definite answer regarding your paid consulting services cannot be provided without a review of the specific facts as various situations arise. Each situation would have to be analyzed under the noncontractual conflicts provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 (the Act), the contractual conflicts provisions contained in section 1090 et seq., as well as the common law doctrine of conflicts of interests.

1.b. Whether your voluntary work and/or participation in various organizations would give rise to a common law incompatibility of office issue would depend on whether the position you occupy in each organization is a "public office".

2.a. If you receive income from a consulting firm for work on recommendations submitted to the Task Force, then any participation by you in deliberations by the AC Transit Board of Directors relating to those recommendations could be considered a conflict of interest under the Act.

2.b. No. If you volunteer your services to the Task Force, none of the conflict of interest statutes would apply since they are all based on a financial interest. Your membership on the Task Force is not a public office and therefore does not present a conflict of interest in violation of the common law doctrine of incompatibility of offices based on the information which has been provided to this office.

DISCUSSION:

In order to answer the various issues which have been raised this opinion will be divided into two parts. The first part will identify the various laws or legal principles which would govern your activities. The second part will apply those principles to the issues which have been raised.

I. APPLICABLE LAWS OR LEGAL PRINCIPLES.

A. CONFLICT OF INTEREST

1. The Political Reform Act of 1974 - Government Code Section 87100 et sequitur.

The Political Reform Act of 1974 (the Act) governs non-contractual conflicts of interest. The conflict of interest provisions are found at Government Code sections 87100 et seq.2

Section 87100 sets forth the general prohibition against misuse of public office where a conflict of interest exists.

"No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest."

A financial interest can arise in a variety of situations. They are identified in section 87103.

"An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

2 All references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.

(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater."

In order for a conflict of interest to arise four elements derived from the above sections must be present: (1) participating in or influencing a governmental decision; (2) reasonable foreseeability that the decision will affect the official's financial interest; (3) the existence of a material financial interest; and (4) the financial effect must be distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

(1) Participation in a governmental decision. 2 OCR, section 18700 includes participating in discussions, responding to comments, advising or making recommendations, voting on a matter, appointing a person, obligating or committing the agency to any course of action, entering into a contractual agreement on behalf of the agency and even determining not to act, unless that determination is made because the official disqualifies him/herself due to a conflict of interest.

(2) Reasonable foreseeability and financial interest.
An effect on an official's financial interest is foreseeable when there is a substantial likelihood that it will ultimately occur as a result of a governmental decision. That likelihood need not be a "certainty", but it must be more than a "mere possibility". Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Commission (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983. The pertinent financial interests involved under section 87103 are contained in subsections (a), (c) and (d) since you are self-employed.

(3) Material financial effect. Generally, the materiality of the financial interest is dependent on the dollar value involved.

a. Direct financial interest. Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations (CCR), section 18702.1 provides that the effect of a decision is material if any person or entity which has been a source of income to the official of $250 or more in the preceding twelve months is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency. Such a direct involvement occurs if the affected person or entity initiates the proceeding, if it is a named party in the proceeding or if it is the subject of the proceeding before the governmental agency. 2 CCR, section 18702.1(b). If a primary consultant to whom you were a subcontractor was involved in any of the above situations, you would have to abstain due to a conflict of interest. For example, the District seeks an RFP for compliance with the Clean Air Act and the contract is awarded to your primary consultant. A conflict would immediately arise.3

b. Indirect financial interest. If a decision of the AC Transit Board indirectly affected a project in which you had an interest as a subcontractor you would not be able to participate in or attempt to influence the decision if your business was effected by the dollar limits identified in 2 CCR section 18702.2. For small businesses subsection (g) sets the impacts at an increase or decrease in gross revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or the decision would cause your business to incur or avoid additional expenses or reduce or eliminate existing expenses by $2,500 or more; or the decision will cause an increase or decrease in value of assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more.

3 A violation of section 1090 also would exist.

c. Nexus. The effect of a decision also is deemed to be material if there is a nexus between the purpose for which the official received $250 or more in income and the governmental decision. 2 CCR section 18702.1(a)(1). Subsection (d) defines "nexus" as follows:

"There is a nexus between the purpose for which an official receives income and a governmental decision if the official receives income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision."

The determination whether a "nexus" exists, like all conflict of interest questions, is fact based. For example, if the District was considering paratransit guidelines which you helped to develop as a paid subcontractor and their passage would assist in the marketing of your business, it could be argued that the required nexus exists. It is possible that a nexus might exist involving work you have done as a volunteer if it can be shown that the approval or denial of the work would affect your business. A definitive answer is difficult to provide since there have been very few rulings by the FPPC in this area.

4. Distinguishable from effect on the public. A conflict of interest can be avoided if the decision affects the official's financial interest in substantially the same manner as it will affect all members of the public or a significant segment of the public. 2 CCR, section 18703.

In summary, a conflict under the Act only exists if an official makes or participates in a decision in which he/she has a financial interest. Additionally, it must be reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the official which is distinguishable from its affect upon the public generally.

B. Common Law.

Although the Political Reform Act would appear to have preempted the common law doctrine against conflict of interest there is no case law holding this position. Until the matter is decided a court could apply both the statutory and common law doctrines in determining a conflict of interest violation.

Director Piras  
Re: Conflict of Interest Opinion  
February 28, 1992  

Under this doctrine "[p]ublic officers are obligated . . . [by virtue of their office], to discharge their responsibilities with integrity and fidelity." Terry v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 206. The doctrine can effect financial and nonfinancial personal interests and would disqualify the official from "taking any part in the discussion and vote regarding" the particular matter. 26 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 5, 7 (1955).

Since it is still an open question whether the courts would apply the common law conflict of interest doctrine in the absence of a statutory violation you should abstain from participating in any AC Transit Board discussion or action concerning any recommendation or action where you believe that you may not be able to meet the common law conflict of interest test.

If a conflict of interest should occur under either the Act or the common law, the existence of the conflict would have to be disclosed on the record and you would have to abstain from participating in or attempting to influence the decision.

C. Section 1090 et sequitur. These provisions govern contractual conflicts of interests. They prohibit officials from being financially interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity or by any body or board of which they are members. Section 1090. Any contract in which a direct conflict exists is void from its inception and cannot be saved. A remote interest, defined in section 1091(b), is permissible if there is a disclosure of the interest and non-participation.

Board members are conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of all contracts executed by the board or an agency under its jurisdiction, even though they disqualify themselves from any and all participation in the making of the contract. Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 645, 649.

D. Incompatibility of office.

The common law prohibits the simultaneous holding of incompatible public offices. In 66 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 176, 177-178 (1983) the

* If an incompatibility of offices exists the public officer forfeits by operation of law the first office and retains the second one. Thus, if an incompatibility existed and you were appointed to your position after being elected to your seat on AC

Director Piras  
Re: Conflict of Interest Opinion  
February 28, 1992  

common law rule was summarized, in part, as follows:

"Offices are incompatible, in the absence of statutes suggesting a contrary result, if there is any significant clash of duties or loyalties between the offices, if the dual office holding would be improper for reasons of public policy, or if either officer exercises a supervisory, auditory, or removal power over the other. (38 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 113 (1961).

In People ex rel. Chapman v. Rapsey (1940) 14 Cal.2d 636 the court held that "[t]wo offices are said to be incompatible when the holder cannot in every instance discharge the duties of each."

However, in order for the common law rule to be applicable the positions in question would have to constitute a "public office". The California Attorney General in 68 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 337 (1985) reviewed the leading case law regarding what constitutes a public office for the purposes of the incompatibility doctrine and concluded at page 342:

"...a public office is a position in government (1) which is created or authorized by the Constitution or some law; (2) the tenure of which is continuing and permanent, not occasional or temporary; (3) in which the incumbent performs a public function for the public benefit and exercises some of the sovereign power of the state."

In addition, if a person merely advises policy makers the courts and California Attorney General have ruled that the position is probably not a public office. 56 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen.556, 561. See also, Parker v. Riley (1941) 18 Cal.2d 83, 87; 42 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 93, 95 (1963).

II. APPLICATION OF LEGAL PRINCIPLES TO THE ISSUES.

There are three different types of decisions which could come before the AC Transit Board of Directors as a result of your consulting work:

1. Decisions which may potentially affect your consulting business;

Transit's Board of Directors you would automatically forfeit your seat on the AC Transit Board.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Would the consulting fees paid directly to Director Piras by SCCTA be considered "income" under the Political Reform Act since SCCTA is a public agency?

2. If so, would she be prohibited from participating in any District Board decision regarding the Dumbarton Bridge private contract if she makes specific recommendations to SCCTA regarding provisions in that contract?

3. Would the answer to question number two change if Director Piras’ recommendations to SCCTA regarding privatization contracts were general in nature rather than specifically addressing currently existing contracts, such as the Dumbarton Bridge contract?

SITUATION NO. 3 - CCCTA STUDY ON SERVICE RELIABILITY

FACTS:

Director Piras would like to bid on a proposal being offered by Central Contra Costa Transit Authority (hereafter "CCCTA") to conduct a study assessing how the agency collects and reports indicators on service reliability. This would include assessing current procedures used to measure schedule adherence for both fixed-route and paratransit service, reviewing the methodology used to determine and record missed trips, reviewing scheduled running times for all routes and evaluating the current BART-bus coordination. In addition, the proposers would be expected to develop recommendations to improve CCCTA performance based on the evaluation.

Both AC Transit and CCCTA compete for funds from Measure C *3, the Federal Transportation Agency and Bridge tolls. In addition, the AC Transit Board periodically deals with issues with respect to BART.

QUESTIONS:

1. If Director Piras’ bid is accepted by CCCTA, would her consulting fees be considered income under the Political Reform Act?

2. If Director Piras is a subcontractor for a primary contractor whose bid is accepted by the CCCTA, would her subcontracting fees be considered income under the Political Reform Act?

3. If the answers to questions one or two are yes, would Director Piras be disqualified from participating in any District Board decisions regarding:

a. The funds which both agencies compete for if the work for CCCTA involves recommendations involving those funds?

b. AC Transit routes which connect to BART stations which may be addressed in the work required for CCCTA?

SITUATION NO. 4 - ADA POLICY COMMITTEE:

FACTS:

Director Piras is an alternate, but active, member of an Americans With Disabilities Act (ADA) Policy Committee (hereinafter "committee") which is comprised of two directors from each of four regional transit agencies, specifically, AC Transit, BART, Union City Transit, and Livermore-Amador Valley Transit Agency (LAVTA). The purpose of the committee is to devise a plan to coordinate ADA service in Alameda County. The plan is to include general provisions applicable to all the agencies as well as provisions specific to each agency. MTC assisted the agencies in negotiating the Memorandum of Understanding which established the committee. The members are not compensated for serving on the committee.

*3. Measure C is a local one-half (1/2) cent sales tax measure for transportation projects approved by Contra Costa County voters in 1988.

One of the operators represented on the committee, LAVTA, acts as a lead and has received funds from MTC to hire a consultant to design the regional ADA plan for the committee to consider. The successful bidder will devise a policy for both the region as well as the individual operators. The bids were solicited by LAVTA which will ultimately determine which consultant to hire. However, a committee made up of staff members from each of the respective regional agencies involved will interview the consultants who bid on the project and will make a recommendation to LAVTA on which bidder to hire.

Presently only three bids for the project have been received. Because of the "minority owned business enterprise" (MBE) and "women owned business enterprise" (WBE) requirements, all three of the primary bidding firms have submitted proposals with subcontracting firms and individuals. Director Piras has connections with two of the proposed bidders as follows:

Bidder No. 1: The prime bidder is currently a subcontractor for Director Piras for a consulting project she is doing for a public agency in Los Angeles County;

Bidder No. 2: Director Piras was a subcontractor for the primary bidder in 1989 before she became a Director at AC Transit. She also had a business association with their subcontractor back in 1989, but no longer considers them competent to do this type of work.

If the staff committee recommends to LAVTA that Bidder No. 2 be selected, Director Piras wants to suggest to the AC Transit Board that it officially withdraw its representation on the committee and petition MTC to receive a certain percentage of the funds allotted to LAVTA to hire its own consultant to work out its own ADA plan in connection with the region.

QUESTIONS:

1. If the regional staff committee decides to recommend that LAVTA accept Bidder No. 2, would Director Piras have a conflict of interest if she participates in an AC Transit Board discussion regarding the recommendation?

2. Would the answer to the above question be different if her business relationship with the primary and subcontractor had occurred within twelve months of the decision?

3. Would the answer to question No. 1 change if the discussion and action concerned Bidder No. 1?

SITUATION NO. 5 - UNIVERSITY OF MASSACHUSETTS FERRY WORKSHOP:

FACTS:

Director Piras has been asked to be a facilitator for a workshop on water transit issues relating to the ADA being put on by the University of Massachusetts. She would be paid an "honorarium" for contributing approximately 23% of the conference agenda. She would also be expected to attend most of the other sessions. Although the University is the responsible entity for the workshop, a private consulting firm is involved in making arrangements for the conference, including soliciting speakers. One of the part-time principals of this private consulting firm is an AC Transit employee. Director Piras has had no contact with this employee and does not know if the employee suggested that she be contacted to be the facilitator of the conference.

QUESTIONS:

1. Would Director Piras have a conflict of interest if she participates in the workshop assuming that her participation is the result of the AC Transit employee's suggestion?

2. Would the "honorarium" that Director Piras would receive from the University of Massachusetts fall within the definition of Title 2, California Code of Regulations, Section 18728, if she is not being paid for speaking as a public official, but instead is being paid for professional and technical services rendered?

If any additional information is required, please feel free to call my office at (510) 891-4827. Thank you for your consideration of these issues.
embedding.first_500_words: December 28, 1992 Fair Political Practices Commission Legal Division 428 "J" Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 RE: Request For A Formal Advice Letter For Director Pat Piras Dear Commissioners: I have been authorized by AC Transit Director Pat Piras to request a formal written advice letter from the Commission pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and Regulation 18329 regarding several potential conflict of interest situations in which she may be involved. The mailing address of Director Piras is listed above. Background Information About AC Transit: AC Transit is a special district created pursuant to PUC Section 24561, et seq. for the purpose of providing mass public transportation to both Alameda and Contra Costa counties. The Board of Directors is composed of seven members, five are elected from wards, and two are elected at large. Director Piras, who was elected to the Board in November, 1990 and represents Ward IV which consists of Castro Valley, the City of San Leandro (except that portion lying north of the San Leandro Creek), San Lorenzo and those portions of the City of Hayward lying generally east of a line following the Southern Pacific tracks southward from the San Leandro city line to West Jackson Street, to I-880, to the Union City limit. The Board is the legislative body of the District which determines all questions regarding District policy. Background Information About Director Piras: Director Piras has a business which provides transportation consulting services to other public agencies, such as counties and cities in the Bay Area and elsewhere in California. Her expertise includes compliance with the Americans With Disabilities Act (ADA) and paratransit issues. She is a sole proprietor but often utilizes other individuals and firms as subcontractors in providing service to public agencies. In addition, she has recurring relationships acting as a subcontractor to other private consulting associations and businesses that provide transportation consulting services to various public sector clients, primarily in California. As a result, Director Piras is often faced with potential conflict of interest situations when the issues she has an opportunity to work on as a consultant or subcontractor dovetail with issues addressed by the Board of Directors at AC Transit. The following are a number factual situations which may present a potential conflict of interest to Director Piras under the Political Reform Act. Each situation is described separately, followed by the questions you are requested to address. SITUATION NO. 1 - ADA TASK FORCE SUB-CONSULTANT WORK FACTS: Director Piras has been asked to participate on the Regional ADA Eligibility Task Force (the Task Force) created by the Metropolitan Transportation Commission (MTC).*1 The members of the Task Force include operators, users and consultants. Participation is strictly voluntary. The purpose of the task force is to develop recommendations for criteria to determine who will be eligible for paratransit service.*2 Once accepted by MTC, the recommendations will be submitted to each fixed route operator for adoption, including AC Transit. The ultimate goal is to have consistent criteria throughout the nine-county Bay
